steps.prompt: Which is the main finite verb?

hints.pati: 'Pati' is an infinitive meaning 'to suffer' — complementary to 'oportuit'
hints.intrare: 'Intrare' is an infinitive meaning 'to enter' — parallel to 'pati'
hints.haec: 'Haec' is a demonstrative pronoun meaning 'these things'

steps.target: to be necessary / to be fitting
choices: to be necessary / to be fitting

hints.to hope: 'To hope' is 'sperare' — 'oportuit' comes from 'oportere' (to be necessary)

steps.reveal: oportuit = it was necessary (3rd sing. perf., impersonal)

hints.present indicative: Present would be 'oportet' — 'oportuit' with -uit is perfect

hints.operor, operari, operatus sum, —: These are the parts of 'operor' (to work) — a deponent verb
hints.opitulor, opitulari, opitulatus sum, —: These are the parts of 'opitulor' (to help) — a deponent verb
hints.opto, optare, optavi, optatum: These are the parts of 'opto' (to wish/choose)

steps.prompt: 'Oportuit' is an impersonal verb. What is its grammatical subject?
steps.target: the infinitive phrase 'Christum pati et intrare'
choices: the infinitive phrase 'Christum pati et intrare'
steps.reveal: oportuit Christum pati = it was necessary for Christ to suffer

hints.'Christum': 'Christum' is accusative — it is the subject of the infinitives, not of 'oportuit'
hints.'haec': 'Haec' is the direct object (these things) of 'pati', not the subject of 'oportuit'
hints.there is no subject: Impersonal verbs have an implied 'it' as subject — the infinitive phrase fills this role

steps.target: accusative — subject of the infinitives 'pati' and 'intrare'
choices: accusative — subject of the infinitives 'pati' and 'intrare'
steps.reveal: Christum is the accusative subject of the infinitive (accusative + infinitive construction)

hints.accusative — direct object of 'oportuit': 'Oportuit' is impersonal — 'Christum' is the subject of the infinitive, not the object of 'oportuit'
hints.nominative — subject of 'oportuit': 'Christum' with -um ending is accusative, not nominative
hints.ablative — agent: Ablative of agent would be 'a Christo' — 'Christum' is accusative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'Christi' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'Christi' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -us — 'Christi' ends in -i

steps.prompt: What is special about the verb 'pati'?
steps.target: it is a deponent verb (passive form, active meaning)
choices: it is a deponent verb (passive form, active meaning)
it is a regular active infinitive
steps.reveal: pati = to suffer (deponent: patior, pati, passus sum)

hints.it is a regular active infinitive: Active infinitive of 3rd conjugation would end in -ere — 'pati' has passive form but active meaning
hints.it is a gerund: A gerund would be 'patiendum' — 'pati' is a deponent infinitive
hints.it is a supine: Supine would be 'passum' — 'pati' is the present infinitive of the deponent 'patior'

hints.pando, pandere, pandi, passum: These are the parts of 'pando' (to spread/open) — note 'passum' looks similar
hints.pasco, pascere, pavi, pastum: These are the parts of 'pasco' (to feed/pasture)
hints.pareo, parere, parui, paritum: These are the parts of 'pareo' (to obey)

steps.reveal: in gloriam suam = into his glory (acc., motion toward)

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'gloria' — 'gloriam' with -am is accusative
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'gloriae' — 'gloriam' is accusative after 'in' (motion)
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'gloria' — 'in' + acc. = motion into

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'gloriae' ends in -ae
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'gloriae' ends in -ae
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'gloriae' ends in -ae

steps.reveal: haec pati = to suffer these things

hints.nominative feminine singular: 'Haec' as nom. fem. sing. is possible, but here it is the object of 'pati' — acc. neut. pl.
hints.nominative neuter plural: Nom. neut. pl. 'haec' looks the same as acc. neut. pl., but as object of 'pati' it must be accusative
hints.ablative: Ablative of 'hic' would be 'his' (pl.) or 'hoc' (sing.) — 'haec' is accusative here

translation: Was it not necessary for Christ to suffer these things and enter into his glory?
